objective: Use the $15M lifetime exemption without the household losing access to the money
reason: The beneficiary spouse can still receive distributions, so the couple keeps indirect access while the assets and all future growth leave both estates.

objective: Create liquidity to pay estate tax without selling the business or the real estate
reason: The death benefit arrives income-tax-free and estate-tax-free at exactly the moment the estate tax bill does.

objective: Move the upside of a volatile, appreciating asset while using little or no exemption
startWith: Grantor Retained Annuity Trust (GRAT) — zeroed out, short term, rolling
reason: Only growth above the IRS assumed interest rate used to value trust (IRS Section 7520 Rate) transfers; if the asset flops, the grantor simply gets it back.

objective: Freeze a large estate built on cash-flowing assets
startWith: Installment sale to an Intentionally Defective Grantor Trust (IDGT)
reason: The estate keeps a note growing at the Applicable Federal Rate (AFR) while the asset's excess growth — and every income-tax payment the grantor makes for the trust — accumulates outside the estate.

objective: Keep wealth compounding for grandchildren and beyond
startWith: Dynasty Trust in a friendly situs (South Dakota, Nevada, Delaware)
reason: Allocating Generation-Skipping Transfer Tax (GST) exemption once shields the assets from the 40% tax at every later generation.

objective: Move an expensive home at a discount while continuing to live in it
reason: The retained right to live there discounts the taxable gift — and the discount is largest when the Section 7520 rate is high, as it is now (5.2%, July 2026).

objective: Exit concentrated low-basis stock, keep an income stream, benefit charity
reason: The tax-exempt trust sells with no immediate capital gain, pays the client for life, and generates an upfront charitable deduction.

objective: Give to charity now but pass the remainder to the children cheaply
startWith: Charitable Lead Annuity Trust (CLAT), often zeroed out
reason: The charity's lead annuity discounts the taxable gift toward zero; performance above the Section 7520 hurdle reaches the heirs tax-free.

objective: Multiply the Qualified Small Business Stock (QSBS) exclusion before an exit
startWith: One or more irrevocable NON-grantor trusts — typically non-grantor dynasty-style children's trusts (see the QSBS Stacking Fit row)
reason: Each non-grantor trust is a separate taxpayer with its own Section 1202 cap — $10M for stock issued on or before July 4, 2025, $15M for stock issued after. Grantor trusts (SLATs, GRATs, standard IDGTs) do NOT stack.

objective: Second marriage — provide for the new spouse, control the remainder
startWith: Qualified Terminable Interest Property (QTIP) Trust (Situational Trusts tab)
reason: The QTIP election secures the marital deduction while the first spouse's plan locks in who ultimately inherits.

objective: Blended family — balance the current spouse against children from a prior marriage
startWith: Qualified Terminable Interest Property (QTIP) Trust with an independent trustee, often alongside separate trusts per family branch
reason: Structure, not hope: the income interest and the remainder interests have opposed incentives, so the referee must be independent and the shares separated.

objective: Surviving spouse is not a U.S. citizen
reason: The only route to the marital deduction for a non-citizen spouse — or the cheaper alternative: naturalization before the estate tax return is filed.

objective: Capture the state estate tax exemption at the first death (especially New York residents)
reason: New York's $7.35M exclusion has no portability — a bypass trust at the first death is the only way to use it, and it sidesteps the 105% cliff.

objective: Protect assets from the client's own future creditors
startWith: Third-party trusts first (a SLAT protects while preserving indirect access); a self-settled Domestic Asset Protection Trust (DAPT) only with eyes open (Situational Trusts tab)
reason: Third-party discretionary trusts rest on settled law; self-settled protection is contested — and California courts do not respect it for California residents.

objective: Provide for a disabled child or heir
startWith: Third-party Special Needs Trust (SNT) (Situational Trusts tab)
reason: Delivers inheritance and support without destroying means-tested benefits — provided distributions supplement rather than replace them.

objective: Estate is safely under the exemption, or the client is elderly and holds low-basis assets
startWith: Often no transfer trust at all — revocable planning, portability election, hold for the basis step-up
reason: Invert first: the free basis step-up at death is worth more than insuring against an estate tax the client will never owe.

objective: Both spouses want to use their exemptions
startWith: Two Spousal Lifetime Access Trusts (SLATs) with deliberately different terms, assets, trustees, and timing
reason: Mirror-image trusts created together risk being unwound under the reciprocal trust doctrine — different is safer than symmetric.

objective: Client hates lifelong complexity and administrative drag
startWith: Fewer, larger, flexible trusts instead of many small special-purpose ones
reason: Every trust is a permanent annual appointment with a CPA and a trustee; small health-and-education silos become lifelong time and cost drains. Consolidation cuts lifetime cost and error surface.
The Section 7520 rate (5.2% in July 2026, up from 4.6%) and the Applicable Federal Rate (AFR) reset monthly; a high rate favors QPRTs and CRTs, a low rate favors GRATs, CLATs, and intra-family notes.
